original_hypothesis.title: Unvalidated Genesis Pool Address Allows Gauge Creation for Arbitrary Pairs
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: GenesisPoolManager.depositToken() does not validate that the genesisPool address parameter was created by the GenesisPoolFactory. An attacker can deploy a malicious contract implementing IGenesisPool that returns true from depositToken(), triggering _preLaunchPool() which creates a real gauge for an attacker-controlled pair address and whitelists an arbitrary token.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The function accepts any non-zero address as genesisPool. A malicious contract can return controlled values from getGenesisInfo() and getLiquidityPoolInfo(), causing gaugeManager.createGauge() to be called with an arbitrary pair address and tokenHandler.whitelistToken() with an arbitrary token.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No factory validation on genesisPool parameter; any address accepted if non-zero

preconditions: Attacker can deploy a contract implementing IGenesisPool interface
gaugeManager.createGauge() does not independently validate the pair was created by pairFactory
No additional access control on depositToken beyond nonReentrant

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deploy FakeGenesisPool returning true from depositToken and attacker-controlled pairAddress from getLiquidityPoolInfo
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Redirect gauge emissions to attacker-controlled pair; whitelist arbitrary tokens for trading

economic_questions: Does gaugeManager.createGauge validate the pool address against the factory?
What emissions can be redirected to a fraudulently created gauge?
Can arbitrary token whitelisting be weaponized for price manipulation?
validation_requirements: Deploy mock FakeGenesisPool and call depositToken through manager
Verify gauge creation succeeds for arbitrary pair address
Quantify emissions redirectable to fraudulent gauge

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas for deploying fake contract + depositToken call (~0.05 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Gauge emissions per epoch directed to attacker's pair
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Very high if emissions are significant
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction to create gauge; ongoing emission theft

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Dilutes legitimate gauge emissions; whitelists potentially malicious tokens

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All legitimate gauge voters and LPs
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced emissions proportional to attacker's fraudulent gauge weight

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify fake pool can trigger full _preLaunchPool flow

validation_result.mechanism: missing input validation on user-supplied address
validation_result.impact_summary: GenesisPoolManager.depositToken() accepts any address as genesisPool without factory validation, enabling an attacker to trigger gauge creation and token whitelisting with arbitrary attacker-controlled values.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The depositToken() function in GenesisPoolManager (lines 112-123) takes an address genesisPool parameter from the caller and performs only two checks: amount > 0 and genesisPool != address(0). It does NOT verify that genesisPool was created by the GenesisPoolFactory. It then calls IGenesisPool(genesisPool).depositToken(msg.sender, amount) on this unvalidated address. An attacker can deploy a malicious contract implementing the IGenesisPool interface that returns true from depositToken() (indicating pre-launch eligibility), returns an attacker-controlled pairAddress from getLiquidityPoolInfo(), and returns an attacker-controlled nativeToken from getGenesisInfo(). When depositToken returns true, the manager calls tokenHandler.whitelistToken() with the attacker's nativeToken and _preLaunchPool() which calls gaugeManager.createGauge() with the attacker's pairAddress. The real GenesisPool.depositToken has an onlyManager modifier, but a fake contract has no such restriction.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 112-123 of GenesisPoolManager.sol clearly show the missing validation. Compare with other functions like approveGenesisPool (line ~97) and rejectGenesisPool (line ~88) which use genesisFactory.getGenesisPool(nativeToken) to get a validated pool address. depositToken() bypasses this pattern entirely by accepting the address directly from the caller.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No validation that genesisPool was created by genesisFactory. Any non-zero address is accepted. An attacker-deployed contract returning true from depositToken triggers _preLaunchPool with attacker-controlled data.
_poolAddress comes from the unvalidated genesisPool. If genesisPool is a malicious contract, _poolAddress is attacker-controlled and used to create a gauge via gaugeManager.createGauge().

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker deploys FakeGenesisPool contract implementing IGenesisPool interface
5. IGenesisPool(genesisPool).depositToken(msg.sender, 1) calls fake contract which returns true
6. preLaunchPool is true, so tokenHandler.whitelistToken(attackerNativeToken) is called
10. transferIncentives called on fake contract (no-op or attacker-controlled)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entry at line 112 accepts user-supplied genesisPool address. At line 116, it calls depositToken on this address - for a fake contract this returns true. At line 118-119, this triggers whitelistToken with attacker-controlled nativeToken and _preLaunchPool with the fake pool. At line 138, _preLaunchPool reads pairAddress from the fake contract. At line 139, gaugeManager.createGauge is called with this attacker-controlled pairAddress.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for GenesisPoolManager initialization, but the vulnerability is in the code logic itself, not in configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is straightforward: (1) Deploy a contract implementing IGenesisPool that returns true from depositToken(), returns attacker-controlled pairAddress from getLiquidityPoolInfo(), and returns attacker-chosen nativeToken from getGenesisInfo(). (2) Call GenesisPoolManager.depositToken(fakePool, 1). (3) The fake pool's depositToken returns true, triggering _preLaunchPool. (4) gaugeManager.createGauge is called with the attacker's pair address. (5) tokenHandler.whitelistToken is called with the attacker's token. The only uncertainty is whether gaugeManager.createGauge has its own validation, but that's out of scope - the calling code has a clear defect. Note that other manager functions (approveGenesisPool at ~line 97, rejectGenesisPool at ~line 88, checkAtEpochFlip at ~line 128) all resolve the genesis pool through genesisFactory.getGenesisPool(), establishing a clear pattern that depositToken violates.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH to CRITICAL. If gaugeManager.createGauge does not independently validate the pair address against the pair factory, an attacker can create gauges for arbitrary (potentially non-existent or malicious) pairs, redirecting protocol emissions. Additionally, tokenHandler.whitelistToken is called with attacker-controlled nativeToken, potentially whitelisting malicious tokens for protocol-wide trading. Even if downstream calls have validation, the missing check represents a clear code defect violating the security pattern used by all other functions in the contract.
recommended_mitigations: Alternative: Change the function signature to accept nativeToken instead of genesisPool, and resolve the pool address internally via genesisFactory.getGenesisPool(nativeToken), matching the pattern used by all other manager functions
Consider adding validation in gaugeManager.createGauge to verify pool addresses against the pair factory as defense-in-depth
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a FakeGenesisPool contract that implements IGenesisPool interface with depositToken returning true, getGenesisInfo returning controlled nativeToken, getLiquidityPoolInfo returning controlled pairAddress, and transferIncentives as a no-op. 2. In a test environment with deployed GenesisPoolManager, call depositToken(fakePoolAddress, 1). 3. Verify that gaugeManager.createGauge was called with the attacker's pair address and tokenHandler.whitelistToken was called with the attacker's token.
uncertainties: Whether gaugeManager.createGauge() has independent validation of the pair address against the pair factory (code not in scope)
Whether tokenHandler.whitelistToken() has access control that would prevent the GenesisPoolManager from whitelisting arbitrary tokens
The exact economic impact depends on whether the downstream gauge/bribe infrastructure processes the fraudulently created gauge
additional_testing_needed: Review gaugeManager.createGauge() implementation to determine if it validates pool addresses
Review tokenHandler.whitelistToken() implementation for access control
End-to-end test with fake genesis pool to verify complete attack path including emission redirection
